text.title: Customer Journey Mapping Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules grounding Customer Journey Mapping. Each pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale; HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override.

rule.statement: Every stage in the map cites at least one piece of qualitative or quantitative evidence — interview quote, ticket ID, or analytics event.
rule.rationale: Evidence-free maps are opinion documents that drift across re-tellings.

rule.statement: The map is co-created with at least one representative from product, design, engineering, and support; solo journey maps drift quickly.
rule.rationale: Single-author maps reproduce that author's assumptions.

rule.statement: Pain points are scored by impact and frequency; the top three become the action backlog with named owners.
rule.rationale: Unprioritised lists rarely turn into work.

rule.statement: Touchpoints are at the channel grain (email, in-app, support call), not feature grain; feature-grain detail belongs in user-flow diagrams.
rule.rationale: Mixing grains makes the map both too detailed and too vague.

rule.statement: An emotion lane is included and populated from real user quotes, not inferred sentiment.
rule.rationale: Inferred emotion reflects team bias, not user reality.

rule.statement: If preconditions in AGENTS.md "Applies If" do not all hold, OR any "Skip If" disqualifier is true, do not run this methodology — route to the named alternative.
rule.rationale: Methodologies are pre-condition gated; running them out-of-scope produces noise and burns the agent context budget.

rule.statement: If preconditions hold, execute the procedure in `content/04-procedure.xml` (or the rule set above when complexity is light), emit the artefact specified by `content/02-output-contract.xml`, and validate with `scripts/validate-journey-mapping.py` before returning.
rule.rationale: Tying the entry rule to the validator script closes the loop between policy and machine check.
